data.title: Returns On Capital Are Showing Encouraging Signs At KPJ Healthcare Berhad (KLSE:KPJ)
data.description:
If we want to find a potential multi-bagger, often there are underlying trends that can provide clues. Typically, we'll want to notice a trend of growing return on capital employed (ROCE) and alongside that, an expanding base of capital employed. Ultimately, this demonstrates that it's a business that is reinvesting profits at increasing rates of return. So on that note, KPJ Healthcare Berhad (KLSE:KPJ) looks quite promising in regards to its trends of return on capital.

Understanding Return On Capital Employed (ROCE)

For those that aren't sure what ROCE is, it measures the amount of pre-tax profits a company can generate from the capital employed in its business. The formula for this calculation on KPJ Healthcare Berhad is:

Return on Capital Employed = Earnings Before Interest and Tax (EBIT) ÷ (Total Assets - Current Liabilities)

0.11 = RM700m ÷ (RM7.8b - RM1.4b) (Based on the trailing twelve months to September 2025).

So, KPJ Healthcare Berhad has an ROCE of 11%. By itself that's a normal return on capital and it's in line with the industry's average returns of 11%.

In the above chart we have measured KPJ Healthcare Berhad's prior ROCE against its prior performance, but the future is arguably more important. If you're interested, you can view the analysts predictions in our free analyst report for KPJ Healthcare Berhad .

What The Trend Of ROCE Can Tell Us

Investors would be pleased with what's happening at KPJ Healthcare Berhad. Over the last five years, returns on capital employed have risen substantially to 11%. The amount of capital employed has increased too, by 37%. So we're very much inspired by what we're seeing at KPJ Healthcare Berhad thanks to its ability to profitably reinvest capital.

The Key Takeaway

In summary, it's great to see that KPJ Healthcare Berhad can compound returns by consistently reinvesting capital at increasing rates of return, because these are some of the key ingredients of those highly sought after multi-baggers. And with the stock having performed exceptionally well over the last five years, these patterns are being accounted for by investors. A look at the shareholders of Ecoeye Co., Ltd. (KOSDAQ:448280) can tell us which group is most powerful. With 72% stake, individual insiders possess the maximum shares in the company. In other words, the group stands to gain the most (or lose the most) from their investment into the company.

As a result, insiders were the biggest beneficiaries of last week’s 16% gain.

What Does The Lack Of Institutional Ownership Tell Us About Ecoeye?

Institutional investors often avoid companies that are too small, too illiquid or too risky for their tastes. But it's unusual to see larger companies without any institutional investors.

There are many reasons why a company might not have any institutions on the share registry. It may be hard for institutions to buy large amounts of shares, if liquidity (the amount of shares traded each day) is low. If the company has not needed to raise capital, institutions might lack the opportunity to build a position. On the other hand, it's always possible that professional investors are avoiding a company because they don't think it's the best place for their money. Institutional investors may not find the historic growth of the business impressive, or there might be other factors at play. You can see the past revenue performance of Ecoeye, for yourself, below.

We note that hedge funds don't have a meaningful investment in Ecoeye. Jongsu Jeon is currently the company's largest shareholder with 23% of shares outstanding. Eunkyung Noh is the second largest shareholder owning 13% of common stock, and Yuchan Jeon holds about 6.7% of the company stock.

On looking further, we found that 56% of the shares are owned by the top 5 shareholders. In other words, these shareholders have a meaningful say in the decisions of the company.

While studying institutional ownership for a company can add value to your research, it is also a good practice to research analyst recommendations to get a deeper understand of a stock's expected performance. Our information suggests that there isn't any analyst coverage of the stock, so it is probably little known.

Insider Ownership Of Ecoeye

The definition of an insider can differ slightly between different countries, but members of the board of directors always count. The company management answer to the board and the latter should represent the interests of shareholders. Notably, sometimes top-level managers are on the board themselves.

Insider ownership is positive when it signals leadership are thinking like the true owners of the company. However, high insider ownership can also give immense power to a small group within the company. This can be negative in some circumstances.

It seems that insiders own more than half the Ecoeye Co., Ltd. stock. This gives them a lot of power. So they have a ₩238b stake in this ₩332b business. Most would be pleased to see the board is investing alongside them. You may wish todiscover (for free) if they have been buying or selling.

General Public Ownership

The general public, who are usually individual investors, hold a 27% stake in Ecoeye. While this size of ownership may not be enough to sway a policy decision in their favour, they can still make a collective impact on company policies.

data.title: Is Poly Property Services Co., Ltd. (HKG:6049) Trading At A 25% Discount?
data.description:
Advertisement

Key Insights

  • Poly Property Services' estimated fair value is HK$44.20 based on 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity
  • Current share price of HK$32.96 suggests Poly Property Services is potentially 25% undervalued
  • The CN¥42.75 analyst price target for 6049 is 3.3% less than our estimate of fair value

How far off is Poly Property Services Co., Ltd. (HKG:6049) from its intrinsic value? Using the most recent financial data, we'll take a look at whether the stock is fairly priced by taking the forecast future cash flows of the company and discounting them back to today's value. One way to achieve this is by employing the Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model. Models like these may appear beyond the comprehension of a lay person, but they're fairly easy to follow.

Companies can be valued in a lot of ways, so we would point out that a DCF is not perfect for every situation. If you want to learn more about discounted cash flow, the rationale behind this calculation can be read in detail in the Simply Wall St analysis model.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

The Model

We use what is known as a 2-stage model, which simply means we have two different periods of growth rates for the company's cash flows. Generally the first stage is higher growth, and the second stage is a lower growth phase. To begin with, we have to get estimates of the next ten years of cash flows. Where possible we use analyst estimates, but when these aren't available we extrapolate the previous free cash flow (FCF) from the last estimate or reported value. We assume companies with shrinking free cash flow will slow their rate of shrinkage, and that companies with growing free cash flow will see their growth rate slow, over this period. We do this to reflect that growth tends to slow more in the early years than it does in later years.

Generally we assume that a dollar today is more valuable than a dollar in the future, so we need to discount the sum of these future cash flows to arrive at a present value estimate:

10-year free cash flow (FCF) estimate

2026202720282029203020312032203320342035
Levered FCF (CN¥, Millions) CN¥1.74bCN¥1.47bCN¥1.46bCN¥1.47bCN¥1.48bCN¥1.51bCN¥1.54bCN¥1.57bCN¥1.61bCN¥1.65b
Growth Rate Estimate SourceAnalyst x3Analyst x2Est @ -0.74%Est @ 0.33%Est @ 1.08%Est @ 1.60%Est @ 1.97%Est @ 2.22%Est @ 2.40%Est @ 2.53%
Present Value (CN¥, Millions) Discounted @ 8.9% CN¥1.6kCN¥1.2kCN¥1.1kCN¥1.0kCN¥967CN¥902CN¥844CN¥792CN¥744CN¥700

("Est" = FCF growth rate estimated by Simply Wall St)
Present Value of 10-year Cash Flow (PVCF) = CN¥10.0b

After calculating the present value of future cash flows in the initial 10-year period, we need to calculate the Terminal Value, which accounts for all future cash flows beyond the first stage. For a number of reasons a very conservative growth rate is used that cannot exceed that of a country's GDP growth. In this case we have used the 5-year average of the 10-year government bond yield (2.8%) to estimate future growth. In the same way as with the 10-year 'growth' period, we discount future cash flows to today's value, using a cost of equity of 8.9%.

Terminal Value (TV)= FCF2035 × (1 + g) ÷ (r – g) = CN¥1.6b× (1 + 2.8%) ÷ (8.9%– 2.8%) = CN¥28b

Present Value of Terminal Value (PVTV)= TV / (1 + r)10= CN¥28b÷ ( 1 + 8.9%)10= CN¥12b

The total value is the sum of cash flows for the next ten years plus the discounted terminal value, which results in the Total Equity Value, which in this case is CN¥22b. The last step is to then divide the equity value by the number of shares outstanding. Compared to the current share price of HK$33.0, the company appears a touch undervalued at a 25% discount to where the stock price trades currently. Valuations are imprecise instruments though, rather like a telescope - move a few degrees and end up in a different galaxy. Do keep this in mind.

Important Assumptions

The calculation above is very dependent on two assumptions. The first is the discount rate and the other is the cash flows. If you don't agree with these result, have a go at the calculation yourself and play with the assumptions. The DCF also does not consider the possible cyclicality of an industry, or a company's future capital requirements, so it does not give a full picture of a company's potential performance. Given that we are looking at Poly Property Services as potential shareholders, the cost of equity is used as the discount rate, rather than the cost of capital (or weighted average cost of capital, WACC) which accounts for debt. In this calculation we've used 8.9%, which is based on a levered beta of 1.166. Beta is a measure of a stock's volatility, compared to the market as a whole. We get our beta from the industry average beta of globally comparable companies, with an imposed limit between 0.8 and 2.0, which is a reasonable range for a stable business.

See our latest analysis for Poly Property Services

SWOT Analysis for Poly Property Services

Strength
  • Earnings growth over the past year exceeded the industry.
  • Currently debt free.
  • Dividends are covered by earnings and cash flows.
    Dividend information for 6049.
Weakness
  • Earnings growth over the past year is below its 5-year average.
  • Dividend is low compared to the top 25% of dividend payers in the Real Estate market.
Opportunity
  • Annual earnings are forecast to grow for the next 3 years.
  • Good value based on P/E ratio and estimated fair value.
Threat
  • Annual earnings are forecast to grow slower than the Hong Kong market.
    What else are analysts forecasting for 6049?

Moving On:

Valuation is only one side of the coin in terms of building your investment thesis, and it is only one of many factors that you need to assess for a company. The DCF model is not a perfect stock valuation tool. Preferably you'd apply different cases and assumptions and see how they would impact the company's valuation. If a company grows at a different rate, or if its cost of equity or risk free rate changes sharply, the output can look very different. Can we work out why the company is trading at a discount to intrinsic value? For Poly Property Services, we've compiled three fundamental items you should explore:

  1. Risks: Consider for instance, the ever-present spectre of investment risk. We've identified 1 warning sign with Poly Property Services , and understanding this should be part of your investment process.
  2. Future Earnings: How does 6049's growth rate compare to its peers and the wider market? Dig deeper into the analyst consensus number for the upcoming years by interacting with our free analyst growth expectation chart.
  3. Other Solid Businesses: Low debt, high returns on equity and good past performance are fundamental to a strong business. Why not explore our interactive list of stocks with solid business fundamentals to see if there are other companies you may not have considered!

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today using a required rate of return. Approach 2: Denali Therapeutics Price vs Book

For companies that are still building toward consistent profitability, the price to book, or P/B, ratio is often a useful yardstick because it compares what you are paying in the market with the accounting value of the net assets on the balance sheet.

• Air Europa has finalized a firm order for up to 40 Airbus A350-900 widebody aircraft, cementing a shift of its long-haul fleet away from Boeing toward Airbus and anchoring its future Spain–Latin America operations.
  • The deal reinforces Airbus’s position in the long-haul widebody market, highlighting airline preference for newer aircraft families that combine range, cargo capability, and passenger comfort on key intercontinental routes.
  • We’ll now examine how Air Europa’s A350-900 commitment, and Airbus’s strengthening role in long-haul fleets, shapes the company’s investment narrative.

What Is Airbus' Investment Narrative?

To own Airbus, you have to believe the company can keep converting a huge order backlog into profitable deliveries while managing supply chain friction, engine issues and tariff uncertainty. The firm’s improving margins, double‑digit earnings growth and high return on equity show that, so far, it has been executing against that backdrop, and the share price still sits below both analyst and discounted cash flow fair value estimates. The newly firmed Air Europa order for up to 40 A350‑900s fits neatly into that story: it deepens Airbus’s role in long‑haul fleets and supports the A350 production ramp, but it is incremental rather than transformational against an already very large backlog, so it is unlikely to change the near term earnings catalysts on its own. Where it might matter more is in reinforcing Airbus’s competitive position in the twin‑aisle market at a time when investors are already watching execution risk closely.

However, the same backlog that supports growth also magnifies any slip in deliveries that investors should be aware of. Despite retreating, Airbus' shares might still be trading 29% above their fair value. Discover the potential downside here.

data.title: Does FDA’s No-New-Trial Stance on Deramiocel Shift the Bull Case for Capricor Therapeutics (CAPR)?
data.description:
• Capricor Therapeutics recently issued a regulatory update on its Biologics License Application for Deramiocel in Duchenne muscular dystrophy, confirming that the FDA has requested the full Phase 3 HOPE-3 clinical study report and supporting data, but no new clinical trials or patient data.
  • The absence of a request for additional studies, combined with Capricor’s plan to submit the HOPE-3 report in February 2026, places the focus squarely on data interpretation and the potential for a new PDUFA action timeline.
  • We’ll now examine how the FDA’s decision not to require new Deramiocel trials shapes Capricor’s investment narrative and future risk profile.

What Is Capricor Therapeutics' Investment Narrative?

For anyone considering Capricor, the big picture still revolves around a simple belief: that Deramiocel can become a meaningful therapy in Duchenne muscular dystrophy and ultimately support a commercial business that justifies today’s valuation swings. The latest FDA update, asking for the full HOPE-3 study report but not new trials, keeps the key near term catalyst squarely tied to how regulators interpret existing data and whether a new PDUFA date is set after the planned February 2026 submission. That is material for the story, because it shifts the focus from clinical risk to regulatory and timing risk. At the same time, the company remains unprofitable, dependent on external funding, and exposed to share price volatility, so execution around the HOPE-3 package and the Nippon Shinyaku partnership matters more than ever.

data.title: Cornerstone Bancorp (OTCPK:CNBP) EPS Growth Outpaces Long Term Trend And Fuels Bullish Narrative
data.description:
Cornerstone Bancorp (CNBP) closed out FY 2025 with fourth quarter total revenue of US$11.0 million and basic EPS of US$3.14, alongside net income of US$3.09 million that capped off a year of steady profitability. The company has seen quarterly revenue move from US$9.66 million in Q4 FY 2024 to US$11.0 million in Q4 FY 2025, while basic EPS over that same period went from US$3.02 to US$3.14, supported by a trailing twelve month net profit margin of 25.9% versus 25.7% a year earlier. Taken together, these numbers point to a business that is holding its margins and giving investors a clear, earnings driven story to assess this latest report.

See our full analysis for Cornerstone Bancorp.

With the headline figures on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up against the widely followed narratives around Cornerstone Bancorp’s growth, quality, and risk.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

TTM earnings growth running at 10.9%

  • Over the last 12 months, Cornerstone Bancorp generated trailing net income of US$10.821 million and basic EPS of US$11.00, with earnings growth of 10.9% versus a 5 year average of 2.4% per year.
  • What stands out for the more bullish narrative is how the 10.9% recent earnings growth and a trailing net profit margin of 25.9% line up with the idea of a steady community bank, yet
    • the five year earnings growth rate of 2.4% per year is much more moderate, so the latest year looks stronger than the longer trend,
    • and quarterly EPS stepping from US$2.44 in Q1 FY 2025 to US$3.14 in Q4 FY 2025 shows a consistent run through the year that supports those bullish arguments about earnings resilience.
Have recent earnings strength really changed the story, or is CNBP still the same slow and steady bank investors expect? 📊 Read the full Cornerstone Bancorp Consensus Narrative.

P/E of 13.6x versus richer peers

  • The shares trade on a trailing P/E of 13.6x, above the peer average of 12.7x and the US Banks industry average of 11.8x, even though the trailing net profit margin only moved slightly from 25.7% to 25.9% over the past year.
  • Skeptical or more bearish views often point to valuation, and that concern is anchored here because
    • the higher P/E multiple means investors are already paying more per dollar of trailing earnings than for the average peer or the broader US Banks group,
    • while the modest change in net profit margin suggests only a small improvement in profitability to support that richer multiple.

DCF fair value above US$221 versus US$150 price

  • The DCF fair value of US$221.20 sits well above the current share price of US$150.00, which implies the stock is about 32.2% below that modelled value even as earnings have grown 10.9% over the last year.
  • Supporters of the more bullish view see this valuation gap as important because
    • the trailing 12 month revenue of US$41.818 million and net income of US$10.821 million show a consistently profitable profile that feeds into the cash flow assumptions behind that DCF fair value,
    • yet the shares being described as highly illiquid over the past three months means that even with this gap, trading can be harder and price moves may not always track the fundamentals closely.

data.title: Is InMode Ltd.'s (NASDAQ:INMD) Recent Stock Performance Tethered To Its Strong Fundamentals?
data.description:
InMode (NASDAQ:INMD) has had a great run on the share market with its stock up by a significant 10% over the last week. Since the market usually pay for a company’s long-term fundamentals, we decided to study the company’s key performance indicators to see if they could be influencing the market. Particularly, we will be paying attention to InMode's ROE today.

Return on equity or ROE is a key measure used to assess how efficiently a company's management is utilizing the company's capital. In short, ROE shows the profit each dollar generates with respect to its shareholder investments.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Advertisement

How To Calculate Return On Equity?

ROE can be calculated by using the formula:

Return on Equity = Net Profit (from continuing operations) ÷ Shareholders' Equity

So, based on the above formula, the ROE for InMode is:

23% = US$150m ÷ US$653m (Based on the trailing twelve months to September 2025).

The 'return' refers to a company's earnings over the last year. Another way to think of that is that for every $1 worth of equity, the company was able to earn $0.23 in profit.

See our latest analysis for InMode

What Is The Relationship Between ROE And Earnings Growth?

So far, we've learned that ROE is a measure of a company's profitability. Based on how much of its profits the company chooses to reinvest or "retain", we are then able to evaluate a company's future ability to generate profits. Assuming everything else remains unchanged, the higher the ROE and profit retention, the higher the growth rate of a company compared to companies that don't necessarily bear these characteristics.

InMode's Earnings Growth And 23% ROE

To start with, InMode's ROE looks acceptable. Further, the company's ROE compares quite favorably to the industry average of 9.8%. This certainly adds some context to InMode's decent 10.0% net income growth seen over the past five years.

As a next step, we compared InMode's net income growth with the industry and found that the company has a similar growth figure when compared with the industry average growth rate of 12% in the same period.

Earnings growth is a huge factor in stock valuation. The investor should try to establish if the expected growth or decline in earnings, whichever the case may be, is priced in. By doing so, they will have an idea if the stock is headed into clear blue waters or if swampy waters await. One good indicator of expected earnings growth is the P/E ratio which determines the price the market is willing to pay for a stock based on its earnings prospects. So, you may want to check if InMode is trading on a high P/E or a low P/E, relative to its industry.

Is InMode Efficiently Re-investing Its Profits?

InMode doesn't pay any regular dividends, meaning that all of its profits are being reinvested in the business, which explains the fair bit of earnings growth the company has seen.

Summary

Overall, we are quite pleased with InMode's performance. Particularly, we like that the company is reinvesting heavily into its business, and at a high rate of return. Unsurprisingly, this has led to an impressive earnings growth. That being so, according to the latest industry analyst forecasts, the company's earnings are expected to shrink in the future. data.title: Is Leidos Holdings (LDOS) Still Attractively Priced After A 96% Three-Year Share Price Gain
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Leidos Holdings at around US$187 per share still offers value after a strong run, this article will walk through what the current price might imply.
  • The stock has delivered a 33.6% return over the past year and 96.3% over three years. More recently it has seen a 2.0% decline over 7 days and a 0.7% return over the last month, with a 2.0% return year to date.
  • Investors have been paying close attention to Leidos because of its role as a major government and defense contractor. Recent headlines have focused on its work across national security, civil, and health programs, and the pipeline of large contracts in these areas. This broader context helps frame why the share price has been active and why sentiment around future funding and contract awards matters.
  • Leidos currently has a valuation score of 5 out of 6, which suggests that on several checks the stock screens as undervalued. Next we will look at how traditional methods like P/E multiples and cash flow models line up, before finishing with a more holistic way to think about what that score really means for you.

Leidos Holdings delivered 33.6% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Professional Services industry.

Approach 1: Leidos Holdings Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes estimates of a company’s future cash flows and discounts them back to today using a required rate of return, giving you an estimate of what the business might be worth per share right now.

For Leidos Holdings, the model uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $1.33b. Analysts provide forecasts out to 2028, and Simply Wall St extends those estimates further using its own assumptions. For example, projected free cash flow is $1.62b in 2026 and $1.71b in 2028, with discounted values provided for each year through 2035 in the model.

When all those projected and discounted cash flows are added together, the DCF model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $290.87 per share. Compared with the current share price of around $187, this implies an intrinsic discount of roughly 35.7%, which indicates that the shares screen as undervalued on this cash flow view.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: Leidos Holdings Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Leidos Holdings, the P/E ratio is a useful way to relate what you pay per share to the earnings the business is currently generating. Investors usually accept a higher P/E when they expect stronger growth or see lower risk, and a lower P/E when they expect slower growth or higher risk.

Leidos trades on a P/E of 17.03x. That sits below the Professional Services industry average P/E of 23.15x and the peer group average of 38.78x. On simple comparisons, the shares look relatively inexpensive compared with both the sector and peers.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Leidos is 24.51x. This is a proprietary estimate of what a more appropriate P/E might be, given the company’s earnings profile, industry, profit margins, market value and risk characteristics. Because it blends these company specific factors, the Fair Ratio can be more tailored than a broad industry or peer average. With the current P/E of 17.03x sitting below the Fair Ratio of 24.51x, Leidos screens as undervalued on this earnings based check.

Result: UNDERVALUED
